context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 8th April, 2024
Days Covered: 13 and 14 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Tree's Degrees [13 points]

Let T be a tree with n ≥ 2 vertices, of which k have degree at least 3.

(a) [8 pts] Prove by induction on n that T has at least k + 2 leaves.
    Hint: Pick a leaf.
(b) [5 pts] Prove the same result using the Handshake Lemma instead of induction.
    Hint: How many edges does a tree with n vertices have?


Problem 2. Froli-King [12 points]

(a) [6 pts] When moving on an 8×8 chessboard, a King can step to any of the ≤ 8 cells that are immediately adjacent to its current cell, either horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. It may be verified that there are 420 possible moves a King can make: 56 moves in each of the four compass directions (N, S, E, W), and 49 moves in each of the four diagonal directions (NE, NW, SE, SW).
Consider placing a King on an otherwise empty chessboard and performing a sequence of consecutive King moves. Say that this sequence is Frolicsome if it (a) starts and ends in the same cell, (b) never performs the same move twice, and (c) never performs a move that it has already done in the opposite direction. Conditions (b) and (c) can be said differently: if the king ever moves from cell c1 to cell c2, then he is not allowed to move from c1 to c2 OR from c2 to c1 for the rest of the walk.
Explain why there does not exist a Frolicsome sequence of King moves consisting of exactly 210 steps.
    Hint: Model this as a graph problem. What are the nodes and edges? Show that a 210-step Frolicsome sequence corresponds to an Euler tour of your graph.
(b) [6 pts] Show that there does exist a Frolicsome sequence of 196 King moves in this 8×8 board. (Show it exists, but don't actually construct it!)
    Hint: Find 14 edges to remove in order to make your graph Eulerian.


Problem 3. DAGs [12 points]

Answer the following questions about the dependency DAG shown in the diagram. Assume each node is a task that takes 1 second.

[Figure: a directed acyclic graph on nodes 1–12. Top row: 1 → 2 → 3, 4, 5, 6, with additional edges 2 → 6, 3 → 6, 5 → 6. Bottom row: 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, with edges 7 → 3, 8 → 3, 3 → 10, 9 → 10, 11 → 12, 10 → 12.]

(a) [2 pts] What is the largest chain in this DAG? If there is more than one, only give one.
(b) [2 pts] What is the largest antichain? (Again, give only one if there are more than one.) Prove there isn't a larger antichain.
(c) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks with a single processor?
(d) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks if there are unlimited processors available?
(e) [2 pts] What is the smallest number p of processors that would still allow completion of all the tasks in optimal time? Show a schedule for p processors that completes in optimal time, and prove that p − 1 or fewer processors must take longer.
(f) [2 pts] What is a topological ordering of these tasks? If there is more than one, only give one.


Problem 4. Pokémon Constraints [13 points]

Ash is selecting a team of Pokémon to use in an upcoming tournament. Each of his Pokémon has a number of types, shown below.

| Pokémon          | Types        |
|------------------|--------------|
| Altaria          | Dragon, Flying |
| Bidoof           | Normal       |
| Chikorita        | Grass        |
| Dratini          | Dragon       |
| Eevee            | Normal       |
| Floragato        | Grass        |
| Grovyle          | Grass        |

Ash has several constraints he must satisfy while selecting his team. We model this task as a graph problem. Let the propositions A, B, C, D, E, F, G be "(Altaria, Bidoof, Chikorita, Dratini, Eevee, Floragato, Grovyle, respectively) is on Ash's team". These propositions and their negations are called literals. Ash's first constraint is that his team must include at least one of Altaria and Eevee, expressed as the 2-clause (A ∨ E).

The two literals in a 2-clause needn't be distinct, and they can be negated. Ash's next two constraints are that his team must include Eevee, and that it cannot include Eevee without Dratini, expressed as the 2-clauses (E ∨ E) and (NOT E ∨ D). Ash's next constraint is that his team must include exactly one of Bidoof and Chikorita, expressed (along with the previous three constraints) as a formula in 2-conjunctive normal form (2-CNF):
    φ1 := (A ∨ E) ∧ (E ∨ E) ∧ (NOT E ∨ D) ∧ (B ∨ C) ∧ (NOT B ∨ NOT C)

(a) [2 pts] Fill in the blanks in the following 2-CNF formula with literals to express the constraint that no two Pokémon on Ash's team may share a type.
    φ2 := (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _)
[The five blanks correspond to the five type-sharing pairs: the Dragon pair, the Normal pair, and the three Grass pairs.]

(b) [3 pts] We can now convert the constraint formula φ := φ1 ∧ φ2 into an implication graph H = (V, I). V := {A, B, C, D, E, F, G, NOT A, NOT B, NOT C, NOT D, NOT E, NOT F, NOT G} is the set of literals, and I encodes the 2-clauses of φ as implications. A 2-clause (u ∨ v) can be written in the logically equivalent form (NOT u ⇒ v) ∧ (NOT v ⇒ u). For every 2-clause (u ∨ v) of φ, I contains the edges (NOT u, v) and (NOT v, u). For instance, I contains the two edges (NOT A, E) and (NOT E, A) to encode (A ∨ E). Draw H or list each vertex's out-neighbors explicitly.
    Hint: H is planar and has 19 edges.

(c) [3 pts] Let ψ be a 2-CNF formula, and let u, v be literals in ψ. Prove that if ψ is true and the implication graph of ψ contains a path π from u to v, then u ⇒ v.
    Hint: Induction on path length.

(d) [3 pts] Identify the SCCs of H (there should be 8). Draw the condensation graph of H, or list each SCC's out-neighbors explicitly.

(e) [2 pts] Find a topological order on the SCCs of H, and identify the set T of literals v for which [NOT v] precedes [v] in this order. Setting these literals to True gives a team that satisfies all of Ash's constraints! What is T, and what is Ash's team?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on Problem 2, especially the Euler tour part. I sort of see that the king moves become a graph, but then I stop knowing what the vertices and edges should be or why 210 is impossible.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Let's build the graph together. Think about what you have on the chessboard: cells and moves between cells. In a graph, you have vertices and edges. Which natural choice maps cells and moves to those two things?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of Problem 2 and needs to build the graph model from scratch. Rather than explaining what the vertices and edges are, I should use a Socratic question to guide them to discover it themselves. The hint in the problem already points to 'model as a graph problem' — I should ask them to think about what the natural objects (cells, moves) correspond to in graph terms. I'll start with the most basic question to get them moving.

exchanges.student: I think the cells are the vertices and a legal king move between two adjacent cells is an edge, but then I’m not sure if that’s too obvious and I still don’t see the 210 contradiction. Could you just explain the whole idea more broadly first?
exchanges.tutor: You're right about vertices and edges — that's exactly correct, not too obvious at all. Now look at the three Frolicsome conditions and think about what they mean for a walk on your graph. What does condition (b) — never performing the same move twice — translate to in graph language?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified vertices and edges. I should affirm this and push them forward rather than giving a broader explanation, which would violate the 'never give the answer directly' rule. The next step is to connect the Frolicsome conditions to properties of a walk in this graph, specifically the Euler tour connection. I'll ask them to think about what the Frolicsome conditions mean in graph terms, one at a time.

exchanges.student: I guess it means I can’t reuse an edge. But I’m still fuzzy because condition (c) feels almost the same, and I’m not seeing how all of that suddenly becomes Euler-tour territory instead of just “some closed walk.” Could you give the big-picture version?
exchanges.tutor: Good — condition (b) means no repeated edge. Now for condition (c): if the king goes from cell c1 to c2, it can't later go c2→c1 either. So both directions of a move are 'used up' at once. Does that suggest the graph should be directed or undirected?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same confusion between conditions (b) and (c). I should help them distinguish the two conditions precisely rather than giving the big picture. Condition (b) says no repeated edge (directed), condition (c) says no edge AND its reverse. This is key to recognizing the graph should be undirected, and then 'no repeated edge in either direction' = each undirected edge used at most once. Once they see that, the Euler tour connection follows naturally. I'll ask a targeted question to distinguish (b) and (c).

exchanges.student: Undirected, I think, since moving between the same two cells counts as the same edge either way. I’m still not connecting that to why exactly 210 steps would force an Euler tour instead of just some cycle that uses a lot of edges.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Now: the problem says there are 420 possible king moves total. Since your graph is undirected, how many undirected edges does it have? And if a Frolicsome walk takes exactly 210 steps without reusing any edge, what fraction of the edges does it use?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly concluded the graph is undirected. Now they need to connect the three Frolicsome conditions together to see that a Frolicsome sequence IS exactly an Euler tour. Condition (a) = closed walk, condition (b)+(c) = each undirected edge used at most once. A 210-step walk uses exactly 210 edges. The graph has exactly 420/2 = 210 undirected edges. So a 210-step Frolicsome sequence uses every edge exactly once — that's an Euler tour. I should ask them to count edges to make this click, rather than spelling it out.

exchanges.student: So 420 directed-looking moves become 210 undirected edges, and a 210-step Frolicsome walk would use all 210 edges exactly once. That didn’t fully clear it up for me; I think I need the whole argument laid out cleanly, including why that means no such sequence exists.
exchanges.tutor: You've got all the ingredients! So a 210-step Frolicsome sequence would be exactly an Euler tour of your graph. Now: what is the condition on vertex degrees that an undirected graph must satisfy for an Euler tour to exist?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly assembled all the pieces: 210 undirected edges, a 210-step Frolicsome walk uses all of them exactly once, and conditions (a)+(b)+(c) together mean it's a closed walk using every edge exactly once — an Euler tour. They're asking me to lay it all out, but I should not do that. Instead, I should point them to the one missing piece: what condition is required for an Euler tour to exist on an undirected graph? That's the Euler tour theorem (all vertices have even degree), and that's what produces the contradiction. I'll ask them about that.

exchanges.student: I remember something like “all vertices have even degree,” but I don’t want to mess up the proof setup. Also I’m kind of mixing this up with Hamiltonian stuff now, so could you just give me a concise full writeup-level explanation for part (a)?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write it up for you — that's your job! But you have the right condition: all vertices must have even degree for an Euler tour to exist. Euler tours are about edges; Hamiltonian paths are about vertices — don't mix them up. Now: do all vertices in your king's graph have even degree? Think about a corner cell versus an edge cell.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give a full writeup, which I must refuse. They have correctly recalled the Euler tour condition (all vertices even degree). I should affirm that and redirect them to apply it: check whether the king's graph actually has all even degrees. That's the final step to the contradiction. I should also briefly clarify the Euler vs Hamiltonian distinction without dwelling on it.

exchanges.student: A corner has degree 3, so not all degrees are even. I’m still worried I’d write something too short or too handwavy though, and I’m also blanking on part (b) about 196 moves—could you explain that one in a broad way instead?
exchanges.tutor: That's the whole argument for part (a) — you have it! Corner cells have odd degree, so the graph has vertices with odd degree, so no Euler tour exists, so no 210-step Frolicsome sequence can exist. Concise and correct is perfectly fine. Try writing that up before moving to part (b). For (b), once you're ready, look at the hint: what does it mean to 'make the graph Eulerian' by removing 14 edges?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has found the key fact: corner cells have odd degree, so not all vertices have even degree, so no Euler tour exists, so no 210-step Frolicsome sequence exists. That's the complete argument for part (a). I should affirm this clearly and push them to write it up rather than moving on prematurely. On the length/handwavy concern, I should reassure them that concise and correct is fine. For part (b), I shouldn't give a broad explanation — I should let them engage with it. But first, I want them to consolidate part (a) before moving to (b).

exchanges.student: I don’t really know what kinds of 14 edges I’m supposed to imagine removing, and “make it Eulerian” still feels vague to me. That approach didn’t click for me, so I want to switch gears and ask about Problem 1 instead—could you just summarize the induction idea there?
exchanges.tutor: Let's stay with Problem 2 for now — you're close on part (b). You already know the graph has vertices with odd degree. Removing an edge affects the degrees of exactly two vertices. So: how many odd-degree vertices does the king's graph have, and what does removing an edge incident to two odd-degree vertices do to the parity of their degrees?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to abandon part (b) and switch to Problem 1. I should not allow this detour — my role is to keep them focused on the current problem. I should gently redirect them back to part (b) and help them make progress. The key question for part (b) is: if removing edges can fix the odd-degree vertices, how many odd-degree vertices are there, and what does removing one edge do to the degrees of its endpoints? This is a concrete question that should help them move forward.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure how many odd-degree vertices there are because I keep second-guessing the board counts. Maybe just tell me the counts by square type and what that implies, because I’m getting lost in the bookkeeping.
exchanges.tutor: You can work this out systematically. The board has three types of cells: corners, non-corner border cells, and interior cells. How many neighbors does a king have in each of those three cases? Once you have the degrees, which types have odd degree?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the counts directly. I should not do that. However, I can help them organize their thinking by asking them to categorize the board squares systematically. The board has corner cells (4), edge cells (non-corner border cells, 24), and interior cells (36). I should ask them to figure out the degree of each type rather than giving the answer. This is straightforward bookkeeping they can do.

exchanges.student: I think corners have 3, non-corner border cells have 5, and interior cells have 8. So the odd-degree ones are corners and non-corner border cells, which I guess is all border cells, but I’m not confident in the total number.
exchanges.tutor: Your degrees are correct! So odd-degree vertices are all border cells. How many border cells does an 8×8 board have in total? Once you have that, think: each edge removal fixes 2 odd-degree vertices — so how many edges do you need to remove?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the degrees right for corners (3) and interior cells (8), but non-corner border cells actually have degree 5, which is correct too. Wait - let me double-check: a non-corner border cell (say on the top edge, not a corner) has 5 neighbors. Yes, that's correct. So odd-degree vertices are corners (degree 3) and non-corner border cells (degree 5). Now they need to count them. There are 4 corners and 24 non-corner border cells, for 28 odd-degree vertices total. I should ask them to count rather than give the number.
